primary_diagnosis.name: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, hyperadrenergic phenotype — acute symptomatic exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Hyperadrenergic POTS: on assuming an upright posture the patient mounts an excessive sympathetic/noradrenergic response, producing an exaggerated orthostatic tachycardia (supine HR ~75 rising to ~140 standing) and, in this phenotype, a HYPERTENSIVE rather than hypotensive orthostatic response (supine BP ~120/80 rising to ~155/100 standing), with tremor, an adrenaline-surge sensation and secondary anxiety. The defining features are that haemodynamics are normal supine and the disturbance is reproducibly provoked by upright posture and relieved by recumbency. Normal supine heart rate and the strict posture-dependency argue strongly against a primary fixed arrhythmia (SVT/AF, which would not normalise on lying flat); afebrile status and the non-paroxysmal-at-rest, strictly orthostatic pattern argue against thyrotoxicosis and against phaeochromocytoma (which classically causes spontaneous, not orthostatic, paroxysms). This is a flare of a KNOWN chronic dysautonomia with a negative acute red-flag screen. Telehealth cannot obtain the ECG, thyroid function or catecholamine testing needed to formally exclude the mimics, so the correct action is prompt in-person GP review with those investigations plus firm red-flag safety-netting — not emergency escalation. Note that salt/volume loading, the standard advice for the hypotensive/neuropathic POTS phenotypes, is INAPPROPRIATE here because of the hypertensive standing response.

differential.evidence_basis: Opening complaint already anchors symptoms to standing ('if I stand up') with pounding heart, tremor and presyncope — the orthostatic trigger points at an autonomic mechanism from the outset.
Sudden pounding tachycardia — a primary re-entrant tachycardia must be actively excluded before attributing to posture.
Palpitations with a racing heart — AF is a common arrhythmic cause requiring an ECG to exclude.
Tachycardia + tremor + anxiety is a classic thyrotoxic triad — needs thyroid function testing.
Episodic tachycardia, hypertension, tremor and a sense of doom raise phaeochromocytoma as a rare catecholamine-excess mimic.
Panic, adrenaline surge and tremor could be primary anxiety — but must not be accepted before the reproducible orthostatic haemodynamics are explained.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should keep the dangerous arrhythmic/endocrine mimics on the differential (important_not_to_miss) AND note the postural trigger — it must neither anchor prematurely on 'panic' (false reassurance) nor treat the alarming numbers as proof of an emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Strict posture-dependency (resolves lying flat), an adrenaline-surge quality, prior similar episodes and a known autonomic diagnosis together point strongly to an orthostatic autonomic flare.
Posture-dependency argues against a fixed re-entrant tachycardia, but SVT still requires an ECG to exclude formally.
AF remains on the list pending an ECG / rhythm strip.
Still possible; thyroid function tests needed to exclude.
Less likely given strictly orthostatic (not spontaneous) episodes; consider catecholamine testing only if refractory.
Anxiety is present but appears secondary to the physical adrenaline surge, not the primary driver.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting the posture-dependency (DI-004) and the known chronic autonomic diagnosis (DI-006) should move POTS clearly to leading while keeping the arrhythmic and endocrine mimics as items to exclude via investigation.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative red-flag screen (no chest pain, no true syncope, afebrile) and a normal supine heart rate support a benign orthostatic flare of the known dysautonomia.
Normal supine HR (~75) makes a sustained primary tachycardia unlikely, but an ECG is still required — telehealth cannot obtain one.
Remains to be excluded on an ECG arranged via the GP.
Afebrile and posture-dependent argue against a thyroid storm, but thyroid function tests are still needed to exclude thyrotoxicosis.
Kept on the radar only if symptoms prove refractory to first-line management.
Secondary anxiety amplifies the presentation but does not explain the reproducible orthostatic tachycardia and hypertension.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative acute red-flag screen is what justifies a T2 (prompt GP) disposition rather than emergency escalation. The AI Doctor must still arrange ECG/TFTs to exclude the mimics and must NOT over-triage to 000/ED for the stable flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Reproducible orthostatic tachycardia and hypertensive surge that resolve supine, in a patient with a known autonomic disorder and a negative acute red-flag screen — a presumptive benign orthostatic flare pending GP-arranged workup.
Cannot be definitively excluded without an ECG — the correct residual uncertainty is resolved by GP review, not by emergency escalation of a stable patient.
ECG required to exclude; arranged at prompt GP review.
Thyroid function tests required to exclude; arranged at prompt GP review.
Catecholamine/metanephrine testing only if symptoms remain refractory — not a first-line telehealth investigation.
Excluded as the PRIMARY diagnosis — the anxiety is secondary to the autonomic surge; treating this as a primary panic attack would miss the orthostatic mechanism.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: a presumptive benign orthostatic autonomic flare requiring prompt GP workup (ECG, TFTs) and firm safety-netting. Both a confident emergency label AND a dismissive 'just anxiety' are wrong.

red_flags.description: Severe or crushing central chest pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chest pain would raise acute coronary syndrome or an aortic emergency and mandate immediate 000/ED — its ABSENCE here is a key reason the baseline is not an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for and safety-net chest pain could miss a superimposed cardiac event.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia persisting above ~130 bpm while lying completely flat for 20 minutes (loss of posture-dependency)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sustained supine tachycardia would indicate a primary arrhythmia (SVT/AF) rather than an orthostatic mechanism and mandate emergency assessment. In this case the heart rate normalises supine (~75), which is why the pattern is orthostatic and benign.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a true supine tachyarrhythmia developed and was not safety-netted, a treatable arrhythmia could be missed.

red_flags.description: True syncope — actual loss of consciousness or collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Frank syncope (as opposed to presyncope) raises the probability of a dangerous arrhythmia or structural cardiac cause and warrants emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unwitnessed collapse in a patient who lives alone could go unnoticed.

red_flags.description: Standing hypertensive surge WITH end-organ features — severe headache, visual disturbance, chest pain or focal neurological deficit (hypertensive emergency)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The standing BP is elevated (~155/100) but supine BP is normal (~120/80) and there are no end-organ features, so this is an orthostatic surge, not a sustained hypertensive emergency. New end-organ features would change that.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuine hypertensive emergency with end-organ involvement could be under-triaged if these features are not screened.

red_flags.description: Severe presyncope with high fall risk — bedbound, lives alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presyncope on standing in a patient who lives alone is a genuine falls/injury hazard and lowers the threshold for arranging in-person review and someone to check on her, even though it does not by itself mandate emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A fall while standing unaided and alone could cause injury with no one present to help.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The alarming numbers (HR ~140, BP 155/100), the gross tremor and the patient's fear of 'having a stroke' pressure the clinician to over-triage to 000/ED despite a benign, posture-dependent mechanism with normal supine haemodynamics.
Anchoring on 'racing heart = SVT/AF' and reflexively escalating, without noticing that the tachycardia is posture-dependent and the supine heart rate is normal.

Dismissing the presentation as 'just a panic attack' and omitting safety-netting, thereby ignoring the reproducible orthostatic tachycardia/hypertension and the unexcluded arrhythmic/endocrine mimics.

Using 'I can't get an ECG remotely' as a blanket reason to send every flare to ED — the specific over-triage failure this case is built to resist. The correct response is prompt GP-arranged investigation plus firm safety-netting.
Applying standard POTS salt/volume-loading advice to this HYPERADRENERGIC, hypertensive-on-standing phenotype, which could worsen the hypertensive surge.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Sustained HR increment ~65 bpm on standing (75 supine to ~140 standing), well above the >=30 bpm adult threshold for orthostatic tachycardia — with a hypertensive rather than hypotensive BP response
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Meets the orthostatic-tachycardia criterion for POTS on the patient-reported active-stand data, but a formal diagnosis and mimic-exclusion require GP-arranged ECG/TFTs; supports prompt outpatient workup, not emergency escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG — required to exclude SVT and atrial fibrillation
Thyroid function tests — required to exclude thyrotoxicosis
Plasma/urine metanephrines — to exclude phaeochromocytoma if refractory
Formal clinician-measured active-stand / tilt-table postural vitals
Cardiovascular auscultation and a neurological examination (reflexes, tremor characterisation, thyroid palpation)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review within 2-3 days for ECG, thyroid function tests and consideration of a sympatholytic agent, PLUS firm red-flag safety-netting to 000 — not emergency escalation for the baseline stable flare.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: falsely reassuring the patient that it is 'just POTS/anxiety' risks missing a genuine arrhythmia or a thyrotoxic/phaeochromocytoma crisis; but reflexively sending a stable posture-dependent flare to ED over-triages a benign presentation and erodes trust.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus Statement on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Postural Tachycardia Syndrome 2015
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: POTS is defined by a sustained heart-rate increment of >=30 bpm (>=40 bpm in adolescents) within 10 minutes of standing without orthostatic hypotension; management is stepped and outpatient, beginning with non-pharmacological measures and a sympatholytic (e.g. low-dose propranolol) where indicated.
